# Extracao: transcription_mp4_01-_When___Why_to_Be_Funny

---

All right, before we learn the strategies, let's talk about when you should be funny. There are some times that are better than others, especially if you're telling a story, delivering a speech, a keynote, graduation, toast, any of those things. There's times to be funny and times not to be funny. So the first rule is don't be funny all the time. Now obviously this rule does not necessarily apply if you're a stand-up comedian. When I'm doing stand-up comedy, I kind of want my audience laughing almost always. But in all other circumstances, I do not. In fact, some of the stories that I tell that are very funny are my least favorite to tell. The last thing I want really is an audience to be laughing the whole time. I want to take them on an emotional journey. And laughing the whole time is not an emotional journey. So don't look for a laugh constantly. Here are some times when you want to look for a laugh. First, if you can make people laugh in the first 30 to 60 seconds that you're speaking, that's tremendously helpful to you. It's a signal to the audience that you know what you're doing. I have already made you laugh. I've only been speaking for 27 seconds and you're all laughing. I obviously am good at what I do. That's confidence building for an audience. There's always this weird dynamic with an audience. As you take the stage or you're standing in front of a conference room, there is concern from the audience that you're going to be terrible at what you're about to do. And that will create an awkward situation for everyone. Nobody wants an awkward situation. So if you can convince them in the first 20, 30, 40 seconds that you know what you're doing, they can relax. They can feel like they're in good hands. And remember, when you make someone laugh, their brain produces three chemicals that improve their mood, their cognition, and the way they feel about the world. And the brain automatically associates those good feelings with the person who has produced it, which is you, which means your audience will automatically like you more as you begin speaking. They'll see you as a more trusted narrator. So laughter in the beginning of a talk, a story, a wedding toast, all of these things can be enormously powerful for you. I also recommend you making people laugh right before you're going to describe something terrible. Just before something terrible is going to happen to you, make them laugh. That enhances the terribleness of the situation that is about to unfold. We want our audience to feel pain when we feel pain. And if you get them to laugh just before the pain happens, it hurts more. I like to think of it this way. If you're getting ready to dump someone, some truly awful person who you've been dating for a little while and you've discovered this is a rotten human being, here's the best way to do it. Take them on the greatest date of their lives. Convince them that the experiences of the day will never be topped. Then dump them. It hurts more that way. Same thing in storytelling and in public speaking. Get people to rise to great heights before you crush their spirits. Laughter before pain always makes pain feel worse, which sounds terrible, but is the goal of storytellers and speakers at times. We want people to feel how awful we felt, so we try to make them feel as awful as we felt. Another time laughter will help is right after those terrible moments. If you have a particularly awful experience that you're describing to an audience, the audience, especially the empathy filled human beings in your audience, the ones who are really good, they're going to go through that experience with you. It's going to be hard for them. And when you finish that experience, they're going to be as torn apart and worn out and distraught as you were. Make them laugh. Let them know through a laugh that everything's OK, that you made it through and they made it through. And we can now move on with the story or the talk or the toast. Make them laugh so they can continue to hear what you have to say next without the suffering that you have just put them through. Those laughs are extremely important. You can also use laughter in the boring parts. There are parts of stories and keynotes and even wedding toasts that get a little boring. There's parts where you have to describe things or explain things. When you have to get into numbers and graphs and all of these details. Those are boring things to say. Sometimes they need to be said. One of the best ways to make those boring things less boring is to make them funny. Find a way to weave humor into the boring parts so they are less boring and people will continue to listen to them. You can also make parts of your story funny if there are no stakes involved. There are parts and stories where sort of nothing is nothing is at stake. Nothing is concerning an audience. There's nothing to worry about. You sort of reach this plateau in a story before you're going to fall off the plateau. You know, those parts and stories where the audience is wondering, why are we still listening? If you are worried about the audience thinking, why are we still listening? Make them laugh. Make them laugh and they will continue to listen. It won't work forever. People will eventually figure out he or she, they're just being funny. They're just being funny, but the story is going nowhere. So you can't use it for long, but you can use it in the place of stakes. Use it in the place of those things and stories that usually keep audiences interested. When you don't have one of those things, make it funny and it'll work better for you. Here's a place never to be funny at the end of your talk, at the end of your story. When you reach the end of a talk, when you reach the end of a story, you don't want to be funny because ideally you've been trying to say something important to the audience. You've been trying to convey an idea. You've been trying to relate and experience from your life. You're trying to teach a lesson. You're trying to make people understand what your life is like. The worst thing you can do is end a story on a pun or a joke or a stupid laugh. People don't listen to stories and talks. They don't listen to keynote addresses and commencement speeches so that at the end they can all laugh while you walk off stage. They listen because at the end they actually want something new in their life. They want to have a new idea, a new thought. They want to feel more connected with the person who's speaking. Maybe they want to look at their life in a new way. You've provided a new lens on the world for them. Don't end with a laugh. I know a storyteller, a really brilliant storyteller who ends every one of his stories with a joke, a pun. He takes us through six or seven or eight minutes of real meaning. We end the story and I feel like I'm right with him. And then he throws out a pun to get one final laugh. How do you want your audience to feel when you're done speaking? Do you want them to think of you as someone who's funny, who can put a few words together in a clever way to create a pun? Or do you want them thinking that is a person who understands me, understands the world, taught me something I did not know, gave me a new view on life? I think you want the latter. Even stand-up comics, the end of their performances, their shows, their sets, they often end on something real. Admittedly, that real thing they end on is often also amusing. But a lot of times it's not. A lot of times the last few lines of a stand-up special are really important and they tend not to be very funny. They're the last impression you can make on an audience. You either end on a rimshot or you end on meaning. I suggest ending on meaning. Be funny until the end of your talk. But in the last 30 seconds, maybe the last minute of your talk, it's not time to be funny anymore. It's time to say something that will linger in the minds and hearts of your audience long after you're done speaking. So choose your spots carefully. Once you get good at being funny, you can probably be funny most of the time. It's not actually hard to be funny about most things. Once you understand the skills and the strategies, once you understand how to deliver the content, being funny is not exceptionally hard. Being funny at the right times, at the right moments for the maximum impact, that is the challenge. Be thoughtful, careful, and decisive about when you choose to be funny. That is the way humor should be deployed in almost everything we do.
